coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode glucose nanosensor as an activatable theranostic platform for cancer cell recognition and cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Hai, Xin
Zhu, Xueying
Yu, Kaixin
Yue, Shuzhen
Song, Weiling
Bi, Sai
coredata.dc:description: Integration of disease diagnosis and therapy is crucial in precise medicine, while the “always on” mode often hinders its clinical applications. Herein, inspired by cascaded catalysis, an integrated dual-mode glucose nanosensor as an activable theranostic platform is developed, which is further exploited for cancer cell recognition and enhanced synergistic therapy of lymph cancer. This nanosensor is prepared through the in-situ growth of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with the synergetic reduction of tannic acid (TA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are further decorated with glucose oxidase (GOx). A cascaded catalytic reaction is triggered by glucose, in which GOx catalyzes the oxidation of glucose into gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (•OH) is further produced with the catalysis of GQDs nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity, resulting in the degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx with the release of Ag+. Accordingly, a “turn-off” colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescence dual-mode glucose nanosensor is fabricated, which is readily applied for cancer cell recognition via fluorescence imaging based on the high glucose level in tumor microenvironment. Moreover, the degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx in response to glucose facilitates the cascades-enhanced synergistic therapy of lymph cancer with the combination of starving-like therapy, metal ion therapy and TA-induce apoptosis. This study highlights a glucose-activated theranostic nanoplatform, which provides a great opportunity for cancer-related biosensing, bioimaging and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose nanosensor
Graphene quantum dots
Cascaded catalysis
Cancer cell recognition
Synergistic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro recognition of cancer cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22076087 and 22004077 ), the Special Funds of the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqn20161028), the Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province ( ZR2020JQ08 ), the Youth Innovation Technology Program of Shandong Province (2019KJC029), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M671988 ), the Collaborative Innovation Program of Jinan ( 2018GXRC033 ) and the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology ( QUSTHX201928 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx. (A) TEM images, (B) AFM images, (C) FT-IR spectra, (D) XRD patterns and (F) CD spectra of AgNPs@GQDs and AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, respectively. Inset in (A) is the size distribution. Inset in (B) is the height profile along the line.

Turn-on fluorometry/turn-off colorimetry dual-mode nanosensor for glucose detection. (A) Schematics of the cascaded catalytic reaction-based dual-mode glucose nanosensor. Inset: FRET effect from GQDs (donor) to AgNPs@GQDs (acceptor). UV–vis absorption spectra of AgNPs@GQDs and fluorescence spectra of AgNPs@GQDs and GQDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx under different concentrations of glucose from 0 to 4 mM. Inset: corresponding colorimetric photographs of (B). (C) Relationship between A/A0 and glucose concentration. Inset: linear calibration curve of A/A0 versus logarithm of glucose concentration (log C). (D) Fluorescence spectra of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx under different concentrations of glucose from 0 to 1 mM. Inset: corresponding fluorescent photographs of (D) under the irradiation of UV 365 nm. (E) Relationship between F/F0 and glucose concentration. Inset: linear calibration curve of F/F0 versus log C. (F) Absorbance variation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx treated with glucose (100 μM) in the coexistence with different interferents (100 μM).

In vitro cancer cell recognition and therapeutic effect of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx on cancer cells. CLSM images of (A) L02 cells, (B) K299 cells and (C) glucose (0.5 mM)-pretreated K299 cells after treated with PBS (control), AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (A = 0.6, λ = 403 nm) and AgNPs@GQDs (A = 0.6, λ = 400 nm) for 6 h, respectively. Scale bar: 50 μm. The viability of (D) L02 cells, (E) K299 cells and (F) glucose (0.5 mM)-pretreated K299 cells after incubated with different concentrations of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx and AgNPs@GQDs for 6 h, respectively. (G) CLSM images and (H) flow cytometry analysis of L02 cells, K299 cells and glucose-pretreated K299 cells after different treatments: PBS (control), AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (A = 0.6, λ = 403 nm) and AgNPs@GQDs (A = 0.6, λ = 400 nm), respectively. Scale bars in Fig. 4D: 50 μm. D1, D2, D3 and D4 in Fig. 4H represent the regions of dead cells, late apoptotic cells, live cells, and early apoptotic cells, respectively.

In vivo antitumor study. (A) Therapeutic schedule of K299-tumor-bearing mice. (B) Tumor volume and (C) body weight of K299-tumor-bearing mice after different treatments as a function of time. (D) Average tumor mass, (E) photos of excised tumors, and (F) H&E and TUNEL staining images of tumor slices after different treatments. Scale bar in: 50 μm *p ˂ 0.05 and ***p ˂ 0.001 (n = 5).

Schematics of in-situ synthesis of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx via synergetic reduction for the construction of turn-on fluorescence/turn-off colorimetry dual-mode glucose nanosensor (A), which is applied as activable theranostic nanoplatform for cancer cell recognition via fluorescence imaging and cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy through combination of starving-like/metal ion therapy/TA-induced apoptosis (B).
Determination results from the proposed turn-off colorimetric nanosensor for glucose analysis in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode glucose nanosensor as an activatable theranostic platform for cancer cell recognition and cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integration of disease diagnosis and therapy is crucial in precise medicine, while the “always on” mode often hinders its clinical applications. Herein, inspired by cascaded catalysis, an integrated dual-mode glucose nanosensor as an activable theranostic platform is developed, which is further exploited for cancer cell recognition and enhanced synergistic therapy of lymph cancer. This nanosensor is prepared through the in-situ growth of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with the synergetic reduction of tannic acid (TA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are further decorated with glucose oxidase (GOx). A cascaded catalytic reaction is triggered by glucose, in which GOx catalyzes the oxidation of glucose into gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (•OH) is further produced with the catalysis of GQDs nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity, resulting in the degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx with the release of Ag+. Accordingly, a “turn-off” colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescence dual-mode glucose nanosensor is fabricated, which is readily applied for cancer cell recognition via fluorescence imaging based on the high glucose level in tumor microenvironment. Moreover, the degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx in response to glucose facilitates the cascades-enhanced synergistic therapy of lymph cancer with the combination of starving-like therapy, metal ion therapy and TA-induce apoptosis. This study highlights a glucose-activated theranostic nanoplatform, which provides a great opportunity for cancer-related biosensing, bioimaging and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Theranostics integrated with diagnosis and therapy within a single nanoplatform has great potential in personalized cancer medicine (Lim et al., 2014; Ling et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). However, the conventional theranostic nanoplatforms often simply combine the therapeutic agents with imaging probes through encapsulation or bioconjugation. In this way, both diagnosis and treatment process are presented as an “always-on” mode (Zhen et al., 2018), in which the theranostic agents can work without the exogenous or endogenous stimuli, resulting in a limited signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in the disease sites and an inevitable side effect on normal cells. Recently, the activatable theranostic platforms have attracted great interest, in which the biomarkers that can trigger the diagnosis and therapy is crucial to distinguish the normal and cancer cells (Dong et al., 2018).

Glucose is one of the indispensable nutrition and energy suppliers for humans, which plays a significant role in organism's metabolism (Xu et al., 2019). The abnormal high levels of blood glucose may not only cause endocrine and metabolic diseases such as diabetes mellitus but also is related to cancers (Jiang et al., 2019). For example, due to the greater need of energy for rapid growth and proliferation, the concentration of glucose in tumor cells is often higher than that in normal cells (Dell' Antone, 2012), which thus can be employed for targeting-free cancer cell screening (Chen et al., 2015). Glucose oxidase (GOx), which can specifically catalyze glucose, has been utilized to construct a variety of glucose biosensors (Jiang et al., 2019). Furthermore, GOx can also participate in non-invasive starvation therapy of cancers through cutting off the source of nutrition and energy. However, the free GOx often suffers from the disadvantages of instability and reduced catalytic activity in biological systems. Thus, the development of support matrix to immobilize GOx is of great significance to increase the rigidity of enzyme molecules and improve their stability and activity. Up to now, although many glucose-related works have achieved tumor monitoring and cancer treatment, the integration of both glucose sensing and cancer theranostics is still a challenge.

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with large surface area as well as good electronic and optical properties can not only provide a stable surface for enzyme immobilization (Petkova et al., 2012), but also be utilized as the nanoprobes for biosensing and imaging (Li and Xu, 2014). Besides, due to the capacity to prevent cell proliferation and induce cell apoptosis and necrosis, AgNPs have also been applied as a potent anticancer agent in cancer therapy (Foulkes et al., 2019). Nevertheless, the instability of bare AgNPs induced by uncontrolled oxidation and subsequent degradation in complex biological media hinders their further applications (Mi et al., 2021). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess the merits of high stability, favorable biocompatibility and unique fluorescence emission (Yan et al., 2019), which have been exploited for the “turn-on” sensing of glucose and relevant species with high sensitivity and minor interference (Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015). More importantly, GQDs can also be used as reducing agent and stabilizer to synthesize metal nanoparticles-GQDs composites (such as AgNPs/GQDs), to protect nanoparticles from aggregation or degradation. The as-prepared AgNPs/GQDs have been applied to the sensing of various analytes such as H2O2 and glucose (Chen et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2018), and the construction of multifunctional cancer therapy nanoplatforms (Hai et al., 2021). However, AgNPs/GQDs still suffer from the problems of low synthesis efficiency and sophisticated operation procedures, which thus limit their applications.

In this work, a glucose-responsive nanocomposite of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is synthesized via in-situ growth of AgNPs under cooperative reduction of TA and GQDs, followed by the decoration of GOx outside (Scheme 1 A). A cascaded catalytic reaction system is constructed, in which GOx catalyzes the oxidation of glucose into gluconic acid (GlcA) and H2O2 (eqn (1)) (Fu et al., 2018), and GQDs with peroxidase-like activity can further catalyze H2O2 to produce •OH (eqn (2)) (Sun et al., 2014). Then, Ag0 is oxidized to Ag + by •OH (eqn (3)), resulting in the effective degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx along with the decrease in UV–vis absorbance of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx and recovered fluorescence of GQDs, which thus facilitate the performance of a dual-mode glucose nanosensor (“turn-off” colorimetry and “turn-on” fluorometry).
(1)
g l u c o s e +
O 2
+
H 2
O
→
G O x
g l u c o n i c
a c i d +
H 2
O 2
(2)
H 2
O 2
→
G Q D s
• O H
(3)
A
g 0
+ • O H → A
g +
+ O
H −

Given the high glucose level in tumor microenvironment, this “turn-on” fluorescence protocol can be applied as an activatable theranostic agent to specific recognition of cancer cells. More importantly, the depletion of intracellular glucose catalyzed by GOx contributes to the starving-like therapy, while the produced H2O2 accelerate the degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx with the release of Ag+ and TA for enhanced metal ions therapy and TA-induced apoptosis, respectively, which thus achieves a cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy of lymph cancer. Overall, the as-constructed dual-mode biosensing nanoplatform can be applied for not only sensitive and selective detection of glucose but also specific recognition of cancer cells with enhanced antitumor efficacy, which holds great potential in cancer theranostics and precise medicine.

Graphite powder, terephthalic acid (TPA), tannic acid, and glucose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). H2O2 was obtained from Aladdin Chem. Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). GOx was ordered from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Deionized water of 18 MΩ cm was used throughout.

Preparation of GQDs: GO was firstly synthesized according to the modified Hummers and Offeman's method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958), and the GO dispersion (10 mg mL−1) was prepared by ultrasonic dispersing in 1 M KOH for 30 min. Then, the GQDs were prepared as follows (Hai et al., 2018a). Briefly, 15 mg terephthalic acid (TPA) was added into 2 mL of GO dispersion. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of H2O2 (30 %) and 3.0 mL of deionized water were added dropwise. The solution was stirred for 15 min and transferred to a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave, followed by heating at 200 °C for 5 h in an electric oven. After cooling to room temperature, the products were filtered through a 0.1 μm microporous membrane to remove the precipitate, and then the unreacted H2O2 were removed via rotary evaporation. The obtained solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 and dialyzed over the deionized water in a dialysis bag (MWCO membranes 100–500 Da) for 3 days to remove the residual reactants. Finally, the powder of GQDs was quantified by lyophilization and re-dispersed in deionized water for further use.

Preparation of AgNPs@GQDs: First, 300 μL of TA (10 mM) was added into 30 mL of AgNO3 solution (1 mM), and 100 μL of GQDs (2 mg mL−1, containing 5 M KOH) was injected subsequently. Then, the mixture was stirred for 2 h under room temperature. Finally, the AgNPs@GQDs solution was dialyzed over the deionized water in a dialysis bag (MWCO membranes 3000 Da) for 3 days to remove the residual reactants.

Preparation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx: The AgNPs@GQDs was diluted to the UV–vis absorbance of 2.0 (λ = 400 nm) with deionized water. Then, 2.0 mg GOx was dissolved into 10 mL of AgNPs@GQDs solution under stirring for 12 h. Finally, the unreacted GOx was removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the collected sediment of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx was re-dispersed into deionized water for further use. Since the concentration of metal nanoparticles is positively associated with their absorbance, thus the characteristic absorbance at 403 nm and 400 nm is adopted as the concentration of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx and AgNPs@GQDs, respectively (Lin et al., 2017).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F field-emission electron microscope operating at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were measured on a Bruker Dimension icon atomic force microscopy under ScanAsyst in air mode (Bruker, Germany). The UV–vis spectra were acquired on a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 1.0 cm optical path. The fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan), which offered a 0.5 cm quartz cuvette. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer from 400 to 4000 cm−1 (Thermo, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu-Kα irradiation (Cu Kα = 1.54 Å) in the 2θ range from 10 to 90°.

The stock solution of glucose (100 mM) was prepared in the deionized water freshly, and the working solutions were obtained by appropriate dilution. The detection of glucose was carried out according to the following steps. Firstly, 200 μL of glucose solution was mixed with 100 μL of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (A = 2.0, λ = 403 nm), followed by the addition of 100 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 9.0). The mixture was then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min, and the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded. The linear calibration curves were obtained by plotting the logarithm of glucose concentration (log C) versus the value of A/A0 and F/F0, respectively. Finally, the concentration of glucose in human serums was detected. The human serum samples were collected from human blood donated by Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University (Qingdao, China). All the experiments involving human samples were conducted in accordance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, which was approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University. All volunteers were given informed consent in conformity to the Ethics Committee guidelines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro recognition of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The normal cells (human normal hepatocytes, L02 cells) and cancer cells (human denatured large cell lymphoma cells, K299 cells) were cultured in DMEM (10 % fetal bovine serum) and RPMI-1640 (10 % fetal bovine serum) for 8 h, respectively. Then, the L02 cells, K299 cells and glucose-pretreated K299 cells were incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (A = 0.6, λ = 403 nm) and AgNPs@GQDs (A = 0.6, λ = 400 nm) for 6 h, respectively. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4), and imaged on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon, Japan). The excitation wavelength was 488 nm and the emission wavelength ranging from 490 to 540 nm was served as the green-channel.

The cytotoxicity was evaluated by a standard CCK-8 assay at 96-well plates. The L02 cells, K299 cells and glucose-pretreated K299 cells were incubated with various concentration of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (A = 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8; λ = 403 nm) and AgNPs@GQDs (A = 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8; λ = 400 nm), respectively. Afterward, 20 μL of CCK-8 solution was injected into the wells containing different cells and co-incubated for 2 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured on a Synergy HTX multi-mode microplate reader (BioTek, USA).

To further assess the selective killing capacity of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx for cancer cells, the L02 cells, K299 cells and glucose-pretreated K299 cells were incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (A = 0.6, λ = 403 nm) and AgNPs@GQDs (A = 0.6, λ = 400 nm) for 6 h, respectively. Then, the cells were washed three times with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) to discard the extra probes. Subsequently, the live and dead cells were stained with Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) kit before CLSM imaging. The flow cytometry assay was investigated using Annexin V-FITC and PI apoptosis detection kit. A Cytomics FC 500 flow cytometer (FCM, Beckman Coulter, USA) was used for the flow cytometry measurements.

All the animal experiments were performed in accordance with the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of State Scientific and Technological Commission, which were further approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University. The Balb/c female nude mice (4-weeks old) were bought from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The xenograft tumor models were obtained by subcutaneously injecting K299 cells (1 × 107 cells in 100 μL of PBS) into the mice on the right hind leg region. When the tumor size reached around 75 mm3, the K299 tumor bearing mice were randomly divided into three groups (n = 5) for different treatments of PBS, AgNPs@GQDs and AgNPs@GQDs-GOx via intratumoral injection every third day for eight times, respectively (dose: 100 μL of AgNPs, A = 4.0). Subsequently, the tumor sizes were monitored every two days and calculated by the formula of (width × width × length)/2. The body weight of each mouse was recorded every three days. After the treatment, the mice were sacrificed, and the tumors were collected to record the final weight. Then, the tumors and major organs (including heart, liver, spleen, kidney and lung) were fixed in 4 % buffered paraformaldehyde for 24 h. After embedding and slicing, the tissues were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and monitored on an optical microscope. Besides, the apoptosis of tumors was investigated using terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase deoxyuridine triphosphate (dUTP) nick-end labeling (TUNEL) assay. The images were recorded with Pannoramic MIDI (3D HISTECH Ltd., Hungary).

An in-situ synergistic reduction method is adopted to synthesize AgNPs by chemically reducing Ag + by TA and GQDs (denoted as AgNPs@GQDs) (Hai et al., 2021). In this composite, GQDs are also employed as a stabilizer to protect AgNPs from aggregation and degradation due to the multiple functional groups in GQDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Nguyen et al., 2018) (Fig. S1). Besides, GQDs can directly provide fluorescence signal without the introduction of additional fluorescent probes. Remarkably, the assembly of TA and GQDs on AgNPs possesses plenty of aromatic structure (Sinduja and John, 2016), which thus provides sufficient binding sites for the immobilization of aromatic molecules with great conjugated structure (Hai et al., 2018a). Thus, GOx, that consists of two identical polypeptide chain subunits with two non-covalently bound flavin adenine dinucleotides (FAD) coenzymes, can be readily immobilized on AgNPs@GQDs via π-π conjugating interaction (He et al., 2012).

The raw GQDs and as-synthesized AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is first characterized by TEM and AFM, respectively (Fig. S2, Fig. 1 A and B and Fig. S3). After modification with GOx, the average size of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx with quasi-spherical morphology is slightly increased from 14.6 nm to 16.1 nm compared with AgNPs@GQDs. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out to further demonstrate the structure and component of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (Fig. 1C). The stretching vibration of O–H (3250 cm−1), COO− (1330 cm−1) and C–O (1120 cm−1) indicates the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx. In comparison with AgNPs@GQDs, the skeletal vibration of CC in aromatic structure shifts to lower frequency in AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (from 1546 to 1551 cm−1) due to the π-π conjugation. In addition, the characteristic bands of GOx (N–H stretching vibration: 3403 cm−1, –CH3: 2987 cm−1 and amide I: 1641 cm−1) are only observed in AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (Tian et al., 2019), further suggesting the successful decoration of GOx on AgNPs@GQDs via π-π conjugation. In addition, the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was carried out to demonstrate the crystalline structure of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (Fig. 1D). The XRD patterns of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx exhibit the characteristic peaks of AgNPs at 2θ = 38°, 44°, 65°, and 78°, belonging to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of metallic Ag0 (JCPDS 04–0783), respectively, which verify the formation of AgNPs (Goudeli and Pratsinis, 2017). The broad peak (23°) is ascribed to the amorphous peak of GQDs, suggesting the binding of GQDs on AgNPs (Hai et al., 2018b). These characteristic peaks are the same as those in AgNPs@GQDs, indicating that the decoration of GOx cannot change the crystal structure and chemical component of AgNPs@GQDs. Moreover, the circular dichroism (CD) spectra show that GOx maintains its secondary structure after binding with AgNPs@GQDs (Fig. 1E). Thus, the catalytic activity of GOx can be reserved for glucose sensing and starvation therapy (Tan et al., 2019). Using the Bradford protein assay kit, the loading efficiency of GOx was measured to be ~20 % (Fig. S4). Besides, the UV–vis absorption spectra of fresh AgNPs@GQDs-GOx and AgNPs@GQDs-GOx after stocking for one month confirm the good stability of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (Fig. S5).

In this assay, the cascaded catalytic reaction is initiated by the GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose into gluconic acid and H2O2 in the presence of O2. Then, H2O2 can be decomposed to •OH catalyzed by GQDs nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity (Fig. S6) (Liu et al., 2019b; Wen et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2013), which further oxidize AgNPs into Ag+. As a result, the AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is degraded with the decrease of UV–vis absorbance (Fig. S7). Meanwhile, the liberated TA and GQDs facilitate the fluorescence recovery, owning to the inhibited Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from GQDs (the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength at 450 nm) to AgNPs@GQDs (the maximum UV–vis absorption wavelength at 400 nm) (Fig. 2 A) (Wang et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2017).

Accordingly, a colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode glucose nanosensor is constructed under the optimal detecting pH (Fig. S8 and S9). For turn-off colorimetric detection mode, as the concentration of glucose increases, the UV–vis absorbance of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx decreases with an obvious color fading (Fig. 2B). A linear relationship is obtained between A/A0 and logarithm of glucose (log C) ranging from 10 μM to 4 mM with a detection limit of 4.78 μM (Fig. 2C and the detailed calculation method is shown in Supporting Information). For turn-on fluorescence sensing mode, the fluorescence of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is enhanced as the concentration of glucose increases, due to the inhibition of FRET from GQDs to AgNPs, which can be visible under the irradiation of UV 365 nm (Fig. 2D). A good linear relationship is achieved between F/F0 and logarithm of glucose concentration (log C) in the range from 10 μM to 1 mM. The limit of detection is calculated to be 7.76 μM (Fig. 2E). Besides, AgNPs@GQDs can also be employed as a universal dual-mode nanosensor for the determination of H2O2 (Fig. S10). To assess the specificity of the proposed dual-mode glucose sensor, the potential interferents of metal ions (Na+ and K+), amino acids (cysteine) and various saccharides (mannose, sucrose, fructose, lactose and maltose) are introduced into the system and detected by the colorimetric method (Fig. 2F). As expected, the A/A0 value of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx decreases after treated with glucose (100 μM), which is not influenced in the presence of coexisting species. Thus, the proposed nanosensor demonstrates an excellent selectivity attributed to the catalytic specificity of GOx towards glucose. Further, the practical feasibility of this nanosensor is assessed by detecting glucose in serums of healthy human (4–7 mM) and diabetes patients (>7 mM), respectively (Table 1 ). The results are consistent well with those obtained using the glucose assay kit (GOx-based method). All these results confirm the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method for glucose analysis.

Due to the fast metabolism and proliferation with higher glucose uptake rate, the tumor microenvironment features a greater glucose level (1.21–2.69 mM) than normal cells (<0.07 mM) (Cheng et al., 2018; Nascimento et al., 2016; Walker-Samuel et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 2B, the AgNPs@GQDs-GOx exhibit an obvious color fading when the concentration of glucose is higher than 100 μM, indicating that the effective degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx in response to glucose. Thus, based on the glucose-responsive performance of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, the proposed probe is preferable to react with cancer cells and exhibits effective degradation via cascaded catalytic reaction. On one hand, the GOx-mediated glucose depletion cuts off the essential energy supply for cancerous cell proliferation, which provides a noninvasive strategy for starving-like therapy of cancers (Zhang et al., 2018). On the other hand, the produced H2O2 from glucose oxidation and the high content of endogenous H2O2 (50–100 μM) in cancer cells can synergistically enhance the yield of •OH (Liu et al., 2019a). Moreover, •OH will promote the oxidation of AgNPs into Ag+, which thus accelerates the degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx along with the recovered fluorescence of GQDs for cancer cell recognition and imaging. It should be noted that the released Ag+ is responsible for metal ion therapy (Zhang et al., 2019), while TA served as an effective anticancer reagent can lead to the apoptotic death of cancer cells through the chromosome condensation and DNA fragmentation (Nagesh et al., 2018; Yue et al., 2019). Therefore, the high content of glucose in cancer cells as an endogenous stimulation makes the proposed multifunctional AgNPs@GQDs-GOx as an intelligent and activatable theranostic agent with “turn-on” mode for the recognition and diagnosis of cancer cells, which further triggers the cascades-enhanced synergistic therapy through the combination of starving-like therapy, metal ion therapy and TA induced apoptosis. In contrast, the low level of glucose and H2O2 in normal cells is inadequate to induce effective degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, which thus cannot activate either the fluorescence imaging or cancer therapy. Therefore, compared with the “always-on” mode, the proposed “turn-on” mode nanotheranostic platform has achieved higher detection SNR as well as lower side effect to normal cells.

To validate the recognition ability of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx probe for cancer cells, the human normal hepatocytes (L02, normal cells) and human denatured large cell lymphoma (K299, cancer cells) are incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx for CLSM imaging, respectively. For L02 cells, neither morphological change nor fluorescence signal is observed (Fig. 3 A). In contrast, the bright green fluorescence can be readily recorded after treating the K299 cells with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (Fig. 3B). In addition, the morphology of cells turns round with shrinkage and size reduction, indicating the death or apoptosis of cells induced by AgNPs@GQDs-GOx. Nevertheless, after incubated with AgNPs@GQDs, nearly no fluorescence is visible in both normal and cancer cells with little morphological changes, implying that AgNPs@GQDs without the decoration of GOx cannot distinguish the cancer cells from the normal ones with the inferior therapeutic effect. These results validate the important role of GOx in cancer cell recognition and selective cell killing. Moreover, glucose as a stimulus is crucial to trigger the recognition and treatment of cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 3C, after the K299 cells are pretreated with glucose and incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, a bright fluorescence image is recorded compared with that of the untreated ones. However, only a weak fluorescence is monitored after treating the K299 cells with AgNPs@GQDs, suggesting an efficient glucose concentration-dependent recognition ability of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx.

The multimodal synergistic therapy of cancers based on the cooperation of different treatments can yield enhanced anticancer effects (Fan et al., 2017). To evaluate the selective and cascades-enhanced synergetic therapeutic effect of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx on cancer cells, the cytotoxicity of normal cells (L02 cells) and cancer cells (K299 cells) treated with different concentrations of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is studied by CCK-8 assay (Fig. 3D and E). The viability of L02 cells remains unchanged even at the absorbance of 0.8, indicating that there is no cytotoxicity of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx to normal cells because of the low intracellular glucose level. Besides, the GQDs on the surface can protect AgNPs from uncontrollable degradation, endowing AgNPs@GQDs-GOx with high biocompatibility. In contrast, the viability of K299 cells decreases gradually as the concentration of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx increases, which is lower than those treated with AgNPs@GQDs under the same concentration. This strong cell-killing capacity of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx can be attributed to the glucose-responsive cascaded catalysis, achieving enhanced synergetic therapy of lymph cancer through the combination of starving-like therapy, metal ion therapy and TA-induced apoptosis. Since AgNPs@GQDs can only react with the intracellular H2O2, the Ag+-based metal ion therapy and TA-induced apoptosis are weakened. Further, when K299 cells are pretreated with glucose and incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, the viability is significantly decreased (Fig. 3F), indicating the glucose-dependent cancer therapeutic ability of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx. In contrast, hardly any decrease is observed in the glucose-pretreated K299 cells under various amount of AgNPs@GQDs, which demonstrates the great significance of GOx-mediated glucose oxidation to the cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy.

To further assess the selectivity of the proposed cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy method, the normal L02 cells, cancerous K299 cells and glucose-pretreated K299 cells are incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, which were further double-stained with Calcein AM (green FL) and PI (red FL) to directly visualize the live and dead cells, respectively (Fig. 3G). The normal cells can keep their viability with exclusively green fluorescence, proving the biosafety of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx under low glucose concentration. As for K299 cells, after incubated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, most of the cells are killed and display a red fluorescence, whereas only a little red fluorescence is monitored in AgNPs@GQDs-treated group. These results are consistent with cytotoxicity assay, which further confirm the high killing capacity of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx towards cancer cell based on the cascaded catalysis-induced enhanced therapy. What's more, the glucose-pretreated K299 cells show a much brighter red fluorescence under AgNPs@GQDs-GOx treatment than the cells treated with AgNPs@GQDs, further demonstrating the key role of GOx-catalyzed glucose oxidation in cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy.

Flow cytometry assays were further carried out to prove the selective anticancer effect of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, in which two dyes of FITC-Annexin V and PI were used to stain the apoptosis and necrotic cells, respectively. The relative populations and the quantitative data for the rates (%) of apoptotic cells are illustrated in Fig. 3H. For normal L02 cells, AgNPs@GQDs-GOx induce a low cell apoptosis rate (<2 %) due to their superior stability and biocompatibility under low glucose concentration. In contrast, when K299 cells are treated with AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, the apoptotic rate is found to be 39 %, while only 9.7 % of apoptotic rate is measured after the cells are treated with AgNPs@GQDs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the more liability of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx to react with cancer cells, in which the cascaded catalysis is initiated for the degradation of nanoprobes and facilitates the enhanced starvation, Ag+-based metal ion therapy and TA-induced cell apoptosis. Moreover, the cells pretreated with glucose show more intense cell apoptosis under the treatment of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx (55.3 %) than AgNPs@GQDs (0.7 %), confirming the glucose-dependent enhanced synergetic therapy. The above results demonstrate the excellent therapeutic effects of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx for lymph cancer based on cascades-enhanced synergetic therapy through combining cancer starving-like therapy via GOx-mediated glucose depletion with Ag+-based metal ion therapy and TA-induced apoptosis via •OH-initiated AgNPs@GQDs-GOx degradation.

The in vivo antitumor efficacy of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is further investigated on K299 tumor-bearing mice. The mice are divided into three groups at random (n = 5), which are treated with PBS (control group), AgNPs@GQDs and AgNPs@GQDs-GOx through intratumor injection, respectively. The therapeutic schedule of tumor-bearing mice is executed via eight injections over 22 days (Fig. 4A). To evaluate the inhibition of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx on tumor growth, the average tumor volume and body weight of mice are monitored every third day. As shown in Fig. 4B, the average tumor volume in AgNPs@GQDs-GOx treated group is much lower than other groups, demonstrating the significantly improved tumor suppression effect of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx based on the proposed cascades-enhanced synergetic treatment. Moreover, the biosafety of this theranostic agent is assessed by monitoring the body weight of tumor-bearing mice (Fig. 4C). No significant weight loss is observed during the treatment process, suggesting almost no side effect of the AgNPs@GQDs-GOx nanoprobes on mice. After the treatment, the tumors from each group are harvested, and the weights and corresponding photographs are acquired to evaluate the therapeutic effect. As shown in Fig. 4D and E, the average weight of tumor from AgNPs@GQDs-GOx treated group is only 0.282 g, which is much lower than that in other groups (PBS: 0.554 g, AgNPs@GQDs groups: 0.491 g), demonstrating the superior therapeutic efficacy of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx on lymph cancer.

Moreover, the antitumor efficacy of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx is evaluated using the hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) staining assay (Fig. 4F). Compared with the PBS- and AgNPs@GQDs-treated groups, the greater apoptotic and necrotic rates with severe structural deformation are observed in the tumor tissue slices from the AgNPs@GQDs-GOx group, verifying the highest antitumor ability due to the glucose-responsive cascades-enhanced synergistic therapy. In addition, the well-demarcated cytoplasm and nuclei are presented in the major organs (including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) after H&E staining, and hardly any histopathological abnormality is observed compared with the PBS group (Fig. S11), suggesting the low toxicity side effect and high biosafety of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, which can be readily applied for selective treatment of cancers in vivo. The above results well demonstrate the feasibility of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx with cascades-enhanced antitumor efficacy for cancer therapy, which holds great promise in the development of activatable theranostic nanoplatform in biomedicine.

In summary, we have developed a simple and highly efficient synergetic reduction method for in-situ synthesis of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx, which is responsive to glucose for the activation of cascaded catalytic reaction, achieving the construction of “turn-off” colorimetric/“turn-on” fluorescence dual-mode glucose nanosensor with high sensitivity and selectivity. In this assay, the decoration of GOx is critical to the glucose-responsive performance due to its catalytic ability for glucose oxidation to generate H2O2, which can be decomposed into •OH by GQDs nanozyme, to promote the oxidation and degradation of AgNPs@GQDs-GOx. Given the high glucose content in tumor microenvironment, this nanosensor is versatilely applied for not only cancer cell recognition but also enhanced synergetic treatment of lymph cancer through combination of starving-like therapy, metal ion therapy and TA-induced apoptosis. Overall, our proposed cascaded catalysis-based activable theranostic nanoplatform provide a promising strategy for accurate diagnosis and effective treatment of cancers, which could motivate the development of precision medicine.

Xin Hai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, preparation. Xueying Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Kaixin Yu: Investigation. Shuzhen Yue: Investigation. Weiling Song: Investigation. Sai Bi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22076087 and 22004077), the Special Funds of the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqn20161028), the Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2020JQ08), the Youth Innovation Technology Program of Shandong Province (2019KJC029), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671988), the Collaborative Innovation Program of Jinan (2018GXRC033) and the Open Project of Chemistry Department of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201928).
